questions.question: Explain the difference between Functional and Non-Functional requirements with examples.
keyPoints: Functional requirements describe specific functions the system must perform (what the system does)
Non-functional requirements describe quality attributes (how the system performs)
Example functional: User can login with username and password
Example non-functional: System should respond within 2 seconds

questions.question: List and explain any four characteristics of a good SRS document.
keyPoints: Correct: Requirements accurately represent stakeholder needs
Unambiguous: Single interpretation, clear meaning
Complete: All requirements are included, no missing functionality
Consistent: No contradictions between requirements
Verifiable: Requirements can be tested to confirm compliance

questions.question: Describe any four techniques used for Requirement Elicitation.
keyPoints: Interviews: Direct questioning of stakeholders
Observations: Watching users perform tasks in their environment
Brainstorming: Group session to generate ideas
Prototyping: Creating mockups to visualize requirements
Document Analysis: Reviewing existing documents and systems

questions.question: What is Requirement Validation? Explain why it is important.
keyPoints: Validation is the process of checking that requirements accurately represent stakeholder needs
It ensures requirements are correct, complete, and consistent before design begins
Helps detect errors early when they are cheaper to fix
Prevents downstream problems like redesign, rework, and project failure
Techniques include reviews, prototyping, and test case development

questions.question: Explain the concept of Stakeholder in requirement analysis. List at least four types of stakeholders.
keyPoints: Stakeholder is any person or group with interest in the software system

Types: End users (will use the system), Customers (pay for the system), Developers (build the system), Project Managers (oversee the project), QA Team (test the system), Regulatory bodies (set compliance requirements)
Identifying stakeholders is crucial for gathering complete requirements

questions.question: What is Requirement Traceability? Explain its importance.
keyPoints: Traceability is the ability to trace requirements throughout the development lifecycle
Forward traceability: linking requirements to design, code, and test cases
Backward traceability: linking design/code back to source requirements
Importance: Ensures all requirements are addressed, helps impact analysis of changes, verifies complete test coverage

questions.question: Explain the four phases of Requirement Engineering.
keyPoints: 1. Elicitation: Gathering requirements from stakeholders through various techniques
2. Analysis: Studying requirements to identify conflicts, ambiguities, and feasibility
3. Specification: Documenting requirements in a formal SRS document
4. Validation: Checking that documented requirements accurately reflect stakeholder needs

questions.question: What is Software Requirements Specification (SRS)? Explain the IEEE 830 standard guidelines for SRS and list any eight quality attributes of SRS.
keyPoints: SRS is a formal document describing all functional and non-functional requirements
IEEE 830 provides guidelines for writing good SRS
Quality attributes: Correctness, Unambiguity, Completeness, Consistency, Verifiability, Modifiability, Traceability
Explain each attribute with examples
Mention SRS should be written for audience (developers, testers, customers)

questions.question: Explain the Requirement Elicitation process. Describe at least five elicitation techniques with advantages and disadvantages of each.
keyPoints: Elicitation is gathering stakeholder needs and converting them into requirements

Techniques: Interviews (direct but time-consuming), Surveys (good for many users but lacks depth), Observation (accurate but expensive), Workshops (collaborative but may have dominance issues), Prototyping (visual but can set wrong expectations), Document Analysis (good context but may be outdated)
Discuss when to use each technique
Mention choosing technique based on project type and stakeholder availability

questions.question: What are Non-Functional Requirements? Explain different categories of Non-Functional Requirements with examples for each.
keyPoints: Non-functional requirements specify quality attributes rather than specific functions

Categories: Performance (response time, throughput), Security (encryption, authentication), Reliability (MTBF, error handling), Usability (user-friendly interface), Maintainability (easy to modify), Portability (works on different platforms), Scalability (handles growth), Availability (uptime percentage)
Provide real-world examples for each category
Explain why NFRs are critical for user satisfaction

questions.question: Discuss the process of Requirement Management and the challenges involved in managing changing requirements.
keyPoints: Requirement Management: Process of controlling changes to requirements throughout project lifecycle
Involves: Change request handling, Impact analysis, Version control, Traceability maintenance
Challenges: Scope creep, Stakeholder conflicts, Evolving business needs, Technical constraints, Budget and time pressures
Strategies: Formal change control process, Regular stakeholder communication, Prioritization techniques (MoSCoW, Kano), Requirements baseline
Emphasize importance of managing changes to avoid project failure
